seoTitle: How to Embed a PDF in an Outlook Email Body
metaDescription: Learn how to embed a PDF file directly in the body of an Outlook email using the Object insertion wizard, so it displays as an inline icon.
intro: A standard attachment sitting in the header can easily get overlooked by a busy recipient. Outlook's Object insertion tool lets you place a PDF directly inside your message text as an interactive icon. This guide covers verifying your message format and using the Object wizard to embed the file inline.

steps.heading: Verify your message format
steps.body: Open a new email, click the Format Text tab, and confirm your composition is set to HTML or Rich Text.

steps.heading: Position your cursor
steps.body: Click into the body text where you want the file to appear, then click the Insert tab.

steps.heading: Open the Object wizard
steps.body: In the Text grouping on the ribbon, click the Object icon to launch the insertion modal.

steps.heading: Choose your file
steps.body: Click the Create from File tab, then click Browse and select your target PDF from your desktop.

steps.heading: Display it as an icon
steps.body: Check the Display as icon option so the file appears as a compact, space-saving asset rather than a large embedded preview.

steps.heading: Finish and verify
steps.body: Click OK to insert the file, then confirm you can type text around the anchored icon cleanly in your layout.

faq.question: Why is the Object option grayed out?
faq.answer: Your message format needs to be set to HTML or Rich Text; Plain Text mode doesn't support object insertion.

faq.question: Can I change the icon or label shown for the file?
faq.answer: Yes, the Change Icon option lets you customize the text label displayed under the document thumbnail.

faq.question: What happens when the recipient clicks the embedded icon?
faq.answer: They can double-click the icon directly in the body text to open the file, rather than downloading it separately.

faq.question: Should I use this method for very large files?
faq.answer: For files over about 20 MB, a cloud storage link is generally a better option than embedding directly.
